title: The nuclear model of the atom
examTip: When describing Rutherford's experiment, link each observation to its conclusion — most go straight through (atom mostly empty space), some deflect (positive nucleus repels), very few bounce back (nucleus is small and dense). Each observation needs its own explanation for full marks.
introTips: Scientists in 1900 didn't know atoms had a nucleus at all — they thought positive charge was spread evenly throughout.
Rutherford's experiment was one of the most surprising in physics — even he said the results were as shocking as firing a shell at tissue paper and having it bounce back.
The nuclear model we use today came from building on Rutherford's work — it took decades to refine.
Proton number (atomic number) tells you the element; mass number tells you the total number of protons and neutrons.
Electrons sit in shells at fixed energy levels — this explains chemical behaviour and how atoms emit light.
intro: How do you figure out what is inside something you cannot see? That was exactly the challenge facing physicists in the early 1900s. The answer — firing tiny particles at atoms and watching where they went — led to one of the most important discoveries in science: the nucleus. This story is not just history; it is a masterclass in how evidence forces scientists to abandon a comfortable model and replace it with a better one. And the nuclear model it produced explains everything from chemical reactions to radioactive decay to the power of the Sun.

keyPoints.heading: Thomson's plum pudding model
keyPoints.content: Before Rutherford, the best model of the atom was Thomson's plum pudding model (1904). J.J. Thomson had already discovered the electron (1897) — a tiny negatively charged particle. He proposed that the atom was a sphere of diffuse positive charge with electrons embedded throughout it — like plums in a pudding. The overall atom was neutral because the negative electrons balanced the spread-out positive charge. This model predicted that if you fired particles at atoms, they would all pass straight through with very slight deflections — the positive charge was too spread out to cause big deflections. This prediction turned out to be almost entirely wrong, which is what made Rutherford's results so dramatic.
writeBullets: Thomson's plum pudding model — atom is a sphere of diffuse positive charge with electrons embedded in it
Thomson discovered the electron in 1897 — a tiny, negatively charged particle
Model predicted: fired particles would pass straight through with only tiny deflections
Atom overall neutral — negative electrons balance the spread-out positive charge
This model was disproved by Rutherford's gold foil experiment

keyTerms.def: Thomson's model: a sphere of diffuse positive charge with electrons embedded throughout, like plums in a pudding
A tiny, negatively charged particle in the atom; discovered by Thomson in 1897

keyPoints.memTip: Plum pudding = positive 'dough' with electron 'plums' dotted through it. Replaced by Rutherford's nuclear model.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Mabel should be able to contrast the plum pudding prediction (all particles go through) with what Rutherford actually found.

keyPoints.heading: Rutherford's gold foil experiment
keyPoints.content: In 1909, Geiger and Marsden (working with Rutherford) fired a beam of alpha particles (positively charged, from a radioactive source) at an extremely thin sheet of gold foil. They detected where the particles went using a screen that flashed when hit. Three key observations and their conclusions: (1) Most alpha particles went straight through → the atom is mostly empty space — the plum pudding model's positive charge is not spread out. (2) Some alpha particles were deflected at small angles → there is a small, dense, positively charged region at the centre that repels some alpha particles. (3) A very small number bounced almost straight back → the positive charge is concentrated in an extremely small, dense nucleus — only direct hits cause the large deflections. This was called the nuclear model.
writeBullets: Alpha particles fired at thin gold foil — detector screen recorded where particles went
Most went straight through → atom is mostly empty space
Some deflected at small angles → small, dense, positive nucleus repels alpha particles
Very few bounced almost back → nucleus is tiny and highly concentrated
Result: plum pudding model abandoned; nuclear model proposed

keyTerms.def: Rutherford's 1909 experiment: alpha particles fired at gold foil; results showed atoms have a tiny, dense, positive nucleus
Atomic model proposed by Rutherford: tiny, dense, positive nucleus at the centre with electrons orbiting in empty space
A particle consisting of 2 protons + 2 neutrons; positively charged; used as the 'probe' in Rutherford's experiment

keyPoints.memTip: Most through = empty space. Some deflect = positive nucleus repels. Few bounce back = nucleus tiny and dense. One observation, one conclusion.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Exam questions often ask 'what does observation X tell us about the structure of the atom?' — Mabel needs to link each result directly to a structural conclusion.

keyPoints.heading: Structure of the nucleus — protons, neutrons and electrons
keyPoints.content: The nuclear model gives us the structure of the atom: a tiny, dense, positive nucleus at the centre, surrounded by electrons in shells with almost all the atom being empty space. The nucleus contains: protons (positive charge, relative mass 1) and neutrons (no charge, relative mass 1). Electrons orbit in shells (relative charge −1, relative mass ~1/1836 ≈ essentially 0). Key numbers: Atomic number (proton number, Z) = number of protons = number of electrons in a neutral atom → determines the element. Mass number (A) = number of protons + number of neutrons. Number of neutrons = mass number − atomic number. Example: carbon-12 has atomic number 6 → 6 protons, 6 electrons; mass number 12 → 12 − 6 = 6 neutrons.
writeBullets: Nucleus — tiny, dense, positive; contains protons and neutrons
Proton — relative charge +1, relative mass 1
Neutron — relative charge 0, relative mass 1
Electron — relative charge −1, relative mass ~0 (negligible); in shells around nucleus
Atomic number (Z) = number of protons = number of electrons (neutral atom)
Mass number (A) = protons + neutrons; neutrons = A − Z

keyTerms.def: The number of protons in the nucleus; determines which element the atom is
The total number of protons and neutrons in the nucleus
A neutral particle in the nucleus; relative mass 1, relative charge 0

keyPoints.memTip: A = protons + neutrons. Z = protons only. Neutrons = A − Z. Electrons = Z (neutral atom).
keyPoints.tutor_note: Mabel should practise reading isotope notation ²³⁸₉₂U — the top number is mass number, the bottom is atomic number.

keyPoints.heading: Electron shells and energy levels
keyPoints.content: Electrons do not orbit randomly — they occupy shells at fixed distances from the nucleus, each corresponding to a fixed energy level. The innermost shell (lowest energy) fills first, then the next, and so on. Electrons can absorb energy to jump to a higher energy level (further from the nucleus) — they become 'excited'. When they fall back down to a lower level, they emit electromagnetic radiation (light) of a specific frequency — this is why each element produces a unique spectrum. This model was refined further by Bohr (1913) and later quantum mechanics. For AQA GCSE, you need to know: electrons are in shells, shells represent energy levels, and transitions between shells involve absorbing or emitting radiation. The arrangement of outer electrons determines how atoms bond chemically.
writeBullets: Electrons occupy shells at fixed energy levels around the nucleus
Innermost shell has lowest energy and fills first
Absorb energy → electron jumps to higher energy level (excited state)
Fall back to lower level → emit electromagnetic radiation of specific frequency
Outer electron arrangement determines chemical bonding behaviour

keyTerms.def: A fixed energy level around the nucleus where electrons reside; inner shells fill first
A fixed amount of energy associated with each electron shell; electrons can jump between levels by absorbing or emitting radiation

keyPoints.memTip: Shells = energy levels. Jump up = absorb energy. Fall down = emit radiation (light).
keyPoints.tutor_note: At GCSE level, Mabel just needs to know electrons are in shells at set energy levels and that transitions emit radiation — full quantum treatment is A-level.

tables.title: Rutherford's gold foil — observations and conclusions
rows: Most alpha particles passed straight through
Some alpha particles deflected at small angles
Small, positively charged nucleus repels alpha particles
A very few bounced almost straight back
Nucleus is tiny, dense and contains almost all the atom's mass

0.question: In Thomson's plum pudding model, where was the positive charge in an atom?
options: Spread evenly throughout a diffuse sphere, with electrons embedded in it
Concentrated in a tiny, dense nucleus at the centre
Only in the electrons, which carried both positive and negative charges
In a ring around the outside of the atom
0.correctFeedback: Correct! In the plum pudding model, positive charge was spread throughout the atom like a pudding, with electrons ('plums') dotted through it.
0.wrongFeedback: The plum pudding model pictured positive charge spread evenly through the whole atom — not in a central nucleus. That nucleus idea came from Rutherford's experiment later.

1.question: In Rutherford's experiment, a very small number of alpha particles bounced almost straight back. What does this tell us?
options: The nucleus is very small and dense with a large positive charge concentrated in it
The atom is mostly empty space with no charge
Alpha particles are negatively charged and attracted to the centre
The gold foil was too thick for most particles to pass through
1.correctFeedback: Exactly right! Only a tiny, dense, positively charged nucleus could deflect an alpha particle back — a spread-out charge would not cause that level of repulsion.
1.wrongFeedback: Large deflections (bouncing back) require a large force — which only a concentrated, dense positive charge can provide. This showed the nucleus is tiny but contains almost all the positive charge.

3.question: An atom has an atomic number of 8 and a mass number of 18. How many neutrons does it have?
3.correctFeedback: Correct! Neutrons = mass number − atomic number = 18 − 8 = 10.
3.wrongFeedback: Neutrons = mass number − atomic number = 18 − 8 = 10. The atomic number counts protons only; you subtract it from the mass number to find neutrons.

commonMistakes: Saying 'the atom is mostly empty space' without explaining what this means in terms of the Rutherford experiment — link it to the observation that most alpha particles went straight through.
Confusing mass number and atomic number — atomic number is protons only (bottom number); mass number is protons + neutrons (top number).
Saying electrons are 'in the nucleus' — they orbit the nucleus in shells; only protons and neutrons are in the nucleus.
Describing Rutherford's experiment as 'light fired at gold' — it was alpha particles (positively charged helium nuclei), not light.
Forgetting that a neutral atom has the same number of electrons as protons — equal to the atomic number.
examTips: For the gold foil experiment, always give three separate observations and match each one to a structural conclusion — one mark per observation-conclusion pair is typical.
To find the number of neutrons: neutrons = mass number − atomic number. Write this formula down before calculating.
When comparing the plum pudding model to the nuclear model, the key difference is where the positive charge is — spread out vs concentrated in a tiny nucleus.
If asked why the plum pudding model was abandoned, explain what result it failed to predict: the large-angle deflections and backscattering of alpha particles.
revisionCardBullets: Thomson's plum pudding model: diffuse positive sphere with electrons embedded throughout
Rutherford fired alpha particles at gold foil — results disproved plum pudding
Most went through → atom mostly empty space
Some deflected → small, positive nucleus repels alpha particles
Very few bounced back → nucleus tiny, dense, concentrated
Electron: −1 charge, mass ~0; in shells around nucleus
Atomic number (Z) = proton number = electron number (neutral atom)
Mass number (A) = protons + neutrons; neutrons = A − Z
Electrons in shells at fixed energy levels — transitions emit/absorb radiation
